title: Vegan scones
subtitle: Coconut oil scones with oat milk, dairy-free
excerpt: Light, dairy-free scones made with solid coconut oil rubbed into flour in place of butter, and oat milk in place of cream or buttermilk. The method is identical to a standard scone: rub, bind, pat, cut. The coconut oil must be completely solid before use.
sourceNotes: Scones made with vegetable fat in place of butter are documented in British domestic cookbooks from the early twentieth century, where dripping and lard-based scones are recorded as economical alternatives to butter. The technique of rubbing solid fat into flour to create a short, crumbly scone is documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and in wartime domestic cooking manuals available through the Internet Archive, where butter substitutes including solid vegetable fats are used in the same proportion and by the same method.

content.text: The method is exactly the same as a butter scone. The only difference is the fat and the liquid. Coconut oil behaves like cold butter in the rub-in stage as long as it is solid, the moment it softens, the texture of the scone changes. Work quickly and keep everything cold.

items.name: Oat milk
items.prepNote: cold, plus a little extra for brushing

content.text: Preheat the oven to 210°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Add the {{coconut-oil}} in small pieces, this is the

. Rub it into the flour with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Pour in the {{oat-milk}} and stir briefly with a fork until the dough just comes together; do not overwork it. Tip onto a lightly floured surface and pat to a height of about 3 cm. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Brush the tops with oat milk and bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and dry on top.
